nodes.name: Constitutional Amendments
nodes.description: The 27 amendments to the U.S. Constitution

items.prompt: Which amendment protects freedom of religion, speech, press, assembly, and petition?
items.explanation:
Ratified 1791 as part of the Bill of Rights
Protects five freedoms: religion, speech, press, assembly, and petition
Applies only to government action — private entities are not bound by the First Amendment
Basis for landmark cases including New York Times v. Sullivan (1964) and Brandenburg v. Ohio (1969)
items.cardBack: Freedom of religion, speech, press, assembly, and petition

items.prompt: Which amendment guarantees the right to keep and bear arms?
items.explanation:
Ratified 1791 as part of the Bill of Rights
References 'well regulated Militia' — courts long debated individual vs. collective right
D.C. v. Heller (2008): Supreme Court confirmed an individual right to possess firearms at home
McDonald v. Chicago (2010) extended Heller to state and local governments
items.cardBack: Right to keep and bear arms

items.prompt: Which amendment prohibits the quartering of soldiers in private homes without consent?
items.explanation:
Ratified 1791 as part of the Bill of Rights
A direct response to British Quartering Acts of 1765 and 1774 that forced colonists to house soldiers
Least litigated of all constitutional amendments — almost never invoked in modern courts
items.cardBack: No quartering of soldiers in private homes without consent

items.prompt: Which amendment protects against unreasonable search and seizure?
items.explanation:
Ratified 1791 as part of the Bill of Rights
Requires warrants based on probable cause, describing the place to be searched and items to be seized
Exclusionary rule: evidence obtained illegally may not be used in court (Mapp v. Ohio, 1961)
Carpenter v. United States (2018) extended 4th Amendment protections to cell phone location data
items.cardBack: Protection against unreasonable search and seizure

items.prompt: Which amendment covers self-incrimination, double jeopardy, due process, and just compensation?
items.explanation:
Ratified 1791 as part of the Bill of Rights
'Pleading the Fifth' = invoking the right against self-incrimination
Double jeopardy: cannot be tried twice for the same crime after acquittal
Takings clause: government must pay 'just compensation' when seizing private property
Grand jury requirement for serious federal crimes also established here
items.cardBack: No self-incrimination, double jeopardy; due process and just compensation

items.prompt: Which amendment guarantees the right to a speedy public trial, impartial jury, and counsel?
items.explanation:
Ratified 1791 as part of the Bill of Rights
Guarantees speedy and public trial, impartial jury, right to confront witnesses, and right to an attorney
Gideon v. Wainwright (1963) required states to provide attorneys to defendants who cannot afford one
Applies to all criminal prosecutions, both federal and state
items.cardBack: Right to speedy public trial, impartial jury, and counsel

items.prompt: Which amendment preserves the right to a jury trial in civil cases exceeding $20?
items.explanation:
Ratified 1791 as part of the Bill of Rights
Preserves the right to a jury trial in federal civil cases where the value exceeds $20
The $20 threshold has never been updated despite inflation
Rarely litigated compared to other Bill of Rights amendments
items.cardBack: Right to jury trial in civil cases over $20

items.prompt: Which amendment prohibits cruel and unusual punishment and excessive bail?
items.explanation:
Ratified 1791 as part of the Bill of Rights
Prohibits excessive bail, excessive fines, and cruel and unusual punishment
Central to debates over capital punishment and prison conditions
Furman v. Georgia (1972) temporarily halted all executions as cruel and unusual
items.cardBack: No cruel and unusual punishment or excessive bail

items.prompt: Which amendment says that rights not listed in the Constitution are still retained by the people?
items.explanation:
Ratified 1791 as part of the Bill of Rights
Prevents the argument that rights not explicitly listed in the Constitution do not exist
Basis for the implied 'right to privacy' invoked in Griswold v. Connecticut (1965)
Often paired with the 10th Amendment in federalism debates
items.cardBack: Rights not listed in the Constitution are retained by the people

items.prompt: Which amendment reserves powers not delegated to the federal government to the states?
items.explanation:
Ratified 1791 as part of the Bill of Rights
Reserves to states (or the people) all powers not explicitly granted to the federal government
Foundation of federalism — the basis for most 'states' rights' arguments
Frequently cited in challenges to federal regulations and mandates
items.cardBack: Powers not delegated to the federal government are reserved to the states

items.prompt: Which amendment bars federal courts from hearing suits against states by citizens of other states?
items.explanation:
Ratified 1795
First amendment to reverse a Supreme Court decision — overruled Chisholm v. Georgia (1793)
Established the doctrine of state sovereign immunity from lawsuits in federal courts
Limits but does not eliminate states' legal accountability
items.cardBack: Federal courts cannot hear suits against states by citizens of other states

items.prompt: Which amendment established separate Electoral College ballots for President and Vice President?
items.explanation:
Ratified 1804
Required after the 1800 election disaster — Jefferson and Burr tied in electoral votes for President
Previously, the runner-up became Vice President (leading to the Jefferson/Burr crisis)
Created the modern ticket-based presidential election system
items.cardBack: Separate Electoral College ballots for President and Vice President

items.prompt: Which amendment abolished slavery?
items.explanation:
Ratified December 6, 1865 — eight months after the Civil War ended
Abolished slavery and involuntary servitude except as punishment for a crime
Lincoln's Emancipation Proclamation (1863) freed slaves in rebel states only — the 13th ended it nationally
Missouri was the last Union state to ratify it; required ratification by former Confederate states as a condition of readmission
items.cardBack: Abolition of slavery

items.prompt: Which amendment established birthright citizenship, equal protection, and due process against state action?
items.explanation:
Ratified 1868 during Reconstruction
Granted citizenship to all persons born or naturalized in the U.S., including formerly enslaved people
Overturned Dred Scott v. Sandford (1857) which denied Black Americans citizenship
Most litigated amendment — basis for Brown v. Board, Roe v. Wade, Obergefell, and hundreds of landmark cases
Four sections: citizenship, due process, equal protection, and penalties for denying voting rights
items.cardBack: Citizenship, equal protection, and due process against state action

items.prompt: Which amendment prohibited denying voting rights based on race?
items.explanation:
Ratified 1870 during Reconstruction
Prohibited denying voting rights based on race, color, or previous condition of servitude
Effectively nullified in the South by Jim Crow laws (poll taxes, literacy tests) until the Voting Rights Act (1965)
First federal guarantee of Black men's right to vote — Black women gained the vote with the 19th Amendment (1920)
items.cardBack: Voting rights cannot be denied based on race

items.prompt: Which amendment authorized the federal income tax?
items.explanation:
Ratified 1913
Authorized Congress to levy a federal income tax without apportioning it among states by population
Passed after the Supreme Court struck down an income tax in Pollock v. Farmers' Loan (1895)
Enacted the same year as the 17th Amendment — both were major Progressive Era reforms
items.cardBack: Federal income tax authorized

items.prompt: Which amendment provided for the direct popular election of U.S. senators?
items.explanation:
Ratified 1913
Provided for direct election of U.S. senators by popular vote; previously chosen by state legislatures
Progressives pushed it to reduce corruption (state legislatures were often bribed to elect senators)
Ratified same year as 16th Amendment — paired Progressive Era reforms
items.cardBack: Direct popular election of U.S. senators

items.prompt: Which amendment enacted Prohibition, banning the manufacture and sale of alcohol?
items.explanation:
Ratified 1919; took effect January 1920
Prohibited the manufacture, sale, and transportation of intoxicating liquors
Gave rise to bootlegging, speakeasies, and organized crime (the Al Capone era)
Lasted 13 years until the 21st Amendment repealed it in 1933
Only amendment ever to be fully repealed
items.cardBack: Prohibition of alcohol

items.prompt: Which amendment granted women the right to vote?
items.explanation:
Ratified August 18, 1920
Prohibited denying the right to vote on account of sex
Suffrage movement victory after 70+ years of activism — Seneca Falls Convention was 1848
Tennessee became the decisive 36th state to ratify; the margin was one vote in the state legislature
items.cardBack: Women's right to vote

items.prompt: Which amendment moved presidential inauguration to January 20 and reduced the lame duck period?
items.explanation:
Ratified 1933
Moved presidential inauguration from March 4 to January 20; congressional terms now begin January 3
Reduced the 'lame duck' period from 4 months to 11 weeks
Known as the 'Lame Duck Amendment'
items.cardBack: Presidential inauguration moved to January 20; reduced lame duck period

items.prompt: Which amendment repealed Prohibition?
items.explanation:
Ratified December 5, 1933
Repealed the 18th Amendment — the only time one amendment has fully repealed another
Franklin Roosevelt championed repeal; ratified by state conventions, not state legislatures (only amendment ratified this way)
Ended 13 years of Prohibition
items.cardBack: Repeal of Prohibition

items.prompt: Which amendment limits presidents to two terms in office?
items.explanation:
Ratified 1951
Limits presidents to two elected terms (or 10 years total if they served part of a prior term)
Passed in reaction to FDR's four terms (1933–1945) — previously only a tradition set by Washington
Nine presidents have served exactly two terms; Eisenhower was the first subject to this amendment
items.cardBack: Presidents limited to two terms in office

items.prompt: Which amendment gave Washington D.C. Electoral College votes?
items.explanation:
Ratified 1961
Grants the District of Columbia three electoral votes for presidential elections
D.C. residents had no presidential vote before this amendment despite living in the nation's capital
D.C. still has no voting representation in Congress (no senators, one non-voting House delegate)
items.cardBack: Washington D.C. receives Electoral College votes

items.prompt: Which amendment abolished the poll tax for federal elections?
items.explanation:
Ratified 1964
Abolished poll taxes as a requirement to vote in federal elections
Poll taxes were used in Southern states since Reconstruction to disenfranchise Black and poor voters
Harper v. Virginia (1966) extended the ban to state elections under the 14th Amendment
items.cardBack: Poll tax abolished for federal elections

items.prompt: Which amendment established presidential succession and disability procedures?
items.explanation:
Ratified 1967
Establishes procedures for: VP vacancy (Section 2), president's voluntary disability (Section 3), and involuntary removal for disability (Section 4)
Invoked when Nixon resigned (Ford nominated VP under Section 2), Reagan's surgery (Section 3)
Section 4 — requiring Cabinet to declare a president unable to discharge duties — was discussed but never invoked regarding Trump
items.cardBack: Presidential succession and disability procedures

items.prompt: Which amendment lowered the voting age to 18?
items.explanation:
Ratified 1971
Lowered the voting age from 21 to 18 in all elections
Passed during the Vietnam War — the argument that those old enough to be drafted should be able to vote
Largest single expansion of voting rights since the 19th Amendment (1920)
Ratified in just 100 days — second fastest ratification of any amendment
items.cardBack: Voting age lowered to 18

items.prompt: Which amendment delays congressional pay raises until after the next election?
items.explanation:
Ratified 1992
Prevents any law varying congressional pay from taking effect until after the next House election
Originally proposed by James Madison in 1789 as part of the Bill of Rights — ratified 203 years later
Longest gap between proposal and ratification of any constitutional amendment
A University of Texas student, Gregory Watson, researched its unratified status and campaigned for its passage
items.cardBack: Congressional pay raises cannot take effect until after the next election
